problem: How many even three-digit integers  have the property that their digits, read left to right, are in strictly increasing order (each digit is greater than the previous digit)?

classified_questions.question: What are the restrictions on the digits of the three-digit integer in order for it to have the desired property?
classified_questions.label: f

classified_questions.question: Why can't any of the digits be zero in the three-digit integer?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: If the largest digit is 4, what are the possible choices for the other two digits?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many different ways can we choose two digits from the set {1, 2, 3}?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: For each choice of the other two digits, is there only one valid order that satisfies the given condition? Why or why not?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Following the same reasoning, what are the possible choices for the other two digits if the largest digit is 6 or 8?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many different ways can we choose two digits from the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} or {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many three-digit integers satisfy the desired property with each of the largest digits (4, 6, and 8)?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the total number of three-digit integers that have the property of strictly increasing digits?
classified_questions.label: b
